candidates.comparison: baseline config (no spec decode, mp executor, no async) vs various configs (spec decode, different executors, async scheduling, preemption, chunked prefill) via check_outputs_equal for exact text/token equality
candidates.oracle: exact text + token ID equality (check_outputs_equal), plus acceptance rate thresholds
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: agree -- check_outputs_equal enforces exact text and token ID equality across configs with different scheduling, preemption, and spec decode settings
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: agree -- different scheduling configs produce different batch compositions and different spec decode verification paths, which change matmul numerics
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: agree -- Not Strong #8: spec decode vs base decode exact matching across configs that change batch geometry (preemption, chunked prefill, different executors). No batch-invariant mode enabled.
candidates.code_snippet:
check_outputs_equal(
    outputs_0_lst=base_outs,
    outputs_1_lst=test_outs,
    name_0=f"baseline=[{baseline_config}], params={params}",
    name_1=f"config=[{test_config}], params={params}",
)

candidates.comparison: baseline config (no spec decode, mp, no async) vs ngram_gpu configs (different executors, async scheduling, preemption, chunked prefill) via check_outputs_equal
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: agree -- check_outputs_equal enforces exact text and token ID equality across configs with different scheduling and spec decode settings
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: agree -- different scheduling configs and spec decode verification paths produce different batch compositions, changing matmul numerics
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: agree -- Not Strong #8: spec decode vs base decode exact matching across configs that change batch geometry. No batch-invariant mode enabled.
candidates.code_snippet:
check_outputs_equal(

    outputs_1_lst=test_outs,

    name_1=f"config=[{test_config}], params={params}",
)

candidates.comparison: batch=1 (non-cascade attention) vs batch=64 (cascade attention), exact text equality
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: agree -- exact string == on generated text
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: agree -- batch=1 takes non-cascade attention path while batch=64 triggers cascade attention, different algorithmic paths for attention computation
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: agree -- Not Strong #6: batch size invariance (BS=1 vs BS=64) without batch-invariant mode. Also Not Strong #7: cascade vs non-cascade attention changing batch geometry.
candidates.code_snippet:
prompts = [example_system_message + prompt] * 64
responses = llm.generate(prompts, sampling_params)
for response in responses:
    assert response.outputs[0].text == ref_output

candidates.comparison: model output text (first 2 chars parsed as int) vs expected variable value, across all prompts with accept_rate=1.0
candidates.oracle: exact integer equality from text extraction (check_answers with accept_rate=1.0)
candidates.fixtures: parametrized by model, batch_size, seed, disable_hybrid_kv_cache_manager
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: agree -- exact integer equality extracted from generated text. The oracle parses first 2 characters as an integer, requiring the model to produce exactly the right number as its first output.
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: agree -- PyTorch numeric changes to attention kernels (especially sliding window attention) could cause the model to recall a different value. accept_rate=1.0 means even one failure breaks the test.
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: agree -- no contract guarantees the model will always produce the exact expected number. This is an emergent capability test sensitive to numeric precision in long-context attention.
candidates.code_snippet:
check_answers(
    indices,
    answer,
    [response.outputs[0].text for response in responses],
    accept_rate=1.0,
)

candidates.comparison: model output text (first 2 chars parsed as int) vs expected variable value, with accept_rate=1.0
candidates.oracle: exact integer equality from text extraction (check_answers with accept_rate=1.0)
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: agree -- exact integer equality extracted from generated text with accept_rate=1.0
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: agree -- PyTorch numeric changes (GELU implementation, attention kernels) could affect recall accuracy. The ROCm skip for torch.compile due to GELU instability demonstrates this exact risk.
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: agree -- no contract guarantees the model will always recall exact variable values. This is a functional correctness test that depends on model behavior sensitive to numeric precision.
candidates.code_snippet:
check_answers(
    indices,
    answer,
    [response.outputs[0].text for response in responses],
    accept_rate=1.0,
)

candidates.review: RECLASSIFY -- Phase 1 missed that this is analogous to Strong Contract #6 (streaming vs non-streaming transport). Both executions deliver the exact same prompt tokens to the same engine with the same sampling params (temp=0). The only difference is delivery timing. No other requests are in flight to change batch composition. The engine must produce the same output regardless of when input chunks arrive, since the final prompt is identical. This is an API transport contract.
candidates.comparison: bunched streaming input delivery vs spaced streaming input delivery, same prompts, same engine, temp=0
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: agree -- exact text equality on generated text
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: disagree -- both executions process the same total prompt in the same engine with temp=0. With no concurrent requests, batch composition is identical. Delivery timing does not change the math.
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: disagree -- Strong Contract #6: streaming vs non-streaming response reconstruction is an API transport contract. The bunched vs spaced difference is only delivery timing of the same input, analogous to streaming transport.

candidates.comparison: LoRA-only LLM vs LoRA+spec-decode LLM, exact text match with 90% threshold
candidates.oracle: exact text equality with 90% match ratio threshold
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: agree -- exact text equality with 90% threshold. While the threshold acknowledges FP noise, it is still a match-ratio based on exact text comparison.
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: agree -- spec decode verification runs matmuls at seqlen num_speculative_tokens+1 vs 1, creating different numeric paths. PyTorch numeric changes could increase mismatch rate beyond 10%.
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: agree -- Not Strong #8: spec decode vs base decode exact text matching without batch-invariant mode. The verification path changes batch geometry.
candidates.code_snippet:
if ref_output.outputs[0].text == spec_output.outputs[0].text:
    matches += 1
...
assert matches > threshold

candidates.comparison: spec decode (speculators model) vs non-spec decode (verifier model), exact text match with 66% threshold, plus GSM8K accuracy
candidates.oracle: exact text match ratio >= 66% plus GSM8K accuracy threshold
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: agree -- the 66% exact text match threshold is a weak oracle. While GSM8K accuracy has margin, exact text comparison is brittle.
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: agree -- spec decode verification runs at different seqlen, creating numeric differences. PyTorch changes could reduce exact match rate below 66%.
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: agree -- Not Strong #8: spec decode vs base decode exact text matching without batch-invariant mode.

candidates.review: RECLASSIFY -- Phase 1 overrated C2. The prompts are trivially simple recall tasks ("The secret beta value is 64. What is the secret beta?" and "42+1=43"). With greedy decoding (temp=0), the probability gap between the correct answer and any alternative is enormous. A PyTorch numeric change would need to fundamentally break the model, not cause minor FP drift, to change the output. The substring check is loose. This is effectively a smoke/liveness test.
candidates.comparison: model output text vs expected substring ("64" and "43")
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: agree -- depends on model generating specific numbers in output text
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: disagree -- the prompts are trivial recall/arithmetic. With greedy decoding, a PyTorch numeric change cannot realistically cause the model to fail to produce "64" when asked "What is the secret beta?" with the answer stated in the prompt. This fails criterion 2.
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: agree -- no contract guarantees specific text output
candidates.code_snippet:
assert "64" in responses[0]
assert "43" in responses[1]
